segments.text: Now the next Uttara is 9.2.21 months. In three ways, men of Northern Kuru, Uttara Kuru,
excel both the Tava Timsa Devas, Devas of the 33, and men of Jambudipa.
That means human beings. In what three? I'll stop here for a moment just to comment.
This Northern Kuru, or Uttara Kuru, is supposed to be one of the four great islands or continents,
which are supposed to surround Mount Simaroo.
Every world system is supposed to have a sun and a Mount Simaroo,
which is surrounded by four continents, to the North is Uttara Kuru, to the South is Jambudipa,
to the East is Puba Diweha, and to the West is Aparagoyana.
Now, at the moment, scientists can't see these four continents,
but perhaps it could be that these in the astral world find material world
that we cannot see with the human eye, just like we cannot see Devas, heavenly beings, and ghosts.
But slowly now, scientists are able to take photographs, of course, using infrared photography,
so slowly they realize there are such things.
So to continue the Sutta, the Buddha said that the men of Uttara Kuru excel in the Tava Timsa Devas.
Tava Timsa Devas are the Devas of the 33, where Saka Deva Raja is the chief,
and men of Jambudipa are the human beings.
In what three? The special qualities are unselfishness, lack of greed, and fixity of lifespan.
Mangs in these three ways, men of Uttara Kuru excel both the Tava Timsa Devas and men of Jambudipa.
Mangs in three ways, the Tava Timsa Devas excel both the men of Uttara Kuru and men of Jambudipa.
In what three? In their celestial life, beauty and happiness.
Mangs in these three ways, the Tava Timsa Devas excel both the men of Uttara Kuru and men of Jambudipa.
monks in three ways the men of Jambudipa excel both the men of Uttarakuru and the

those things that we desire then we take up the stick and the sword and we

this or in the sphere of infinite space if perceptions and thoughts accompanied
by form we set him or in the sphere of infinite consciousness if perceptions and
thoughts accompanied by the sphere of infinite space beset him. Or in the sphere of nothingness,
if perceptions and thoughts accompanied by the sphere of infinite consciousness beset
him. Or in the sphere of neither perception nor non-perception, if perceptions and thoughts
accompanied by the sphere of nothingness beset him. It is indeed for him a disease,
still, amounting to a disease might arise in a happy person. Even so, those things
beset him, and indeed they are for him diseases. Now disease is called ill by the exalted one.
Verily, Reverend Sir, it is just in this way that the cool ought to be understood as happiness.
And then Reverend Sir, consider the monk who, passing wholly beyond the sphere of neither
perception nor non-perception, enters and abides in the ending of perception and feeling. When
by wisdom, he sees that the asavas are completely destroyed. Verily, Reverend Sir, it is just in
this way that the cool ought to be understood as happiness, as the end of the sutta. So,
similarly, Verily, Sarikutta is saying, if a person abides in the jhana, and some perceptions
and thoughts accompanied by a lower state arises so that it moves him or ousts him out of that
jhana state, it is just like a happy man becoming sick. He is moved out of that blissful state.
So, those things that move him out of the blissful state are diseases. So, that is why the
available Sarikutta is giving the simile, that when a monk abides in the jhana, it is such a
blissful happy state, similar to nibbana in a way. So, you can see the arahants and the Buddha
themselves, they sometimes liken the jhana states to nibbana.
